content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

December 12, 2017

Roland Simpson
32757 Rincon Ranch Road
P. O. Box 1388
Pauma Valley, CA 92061

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-258

Dear Mr. Simpson:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ We are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

Please note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice. In addition, the Fair Political Practices Commission does not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A).) Therefore, nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions.

QUESTION

Do you have a disqualifying conflict of interest in decisions regarding the reimbursement of a water district's costs for repairing a road from a community services district that is responsible for maintaining the road considering that you serve on the board of directors for both districts and have interests in an avocado farm and property within the two districts?

CONCLUSION

The Act does not prohibit you from taking part in the decision because there is no foreseeable material effect on your business or property. However, we caution that other restrictions may apply including but not limited to any restrictions your agencies may impose on incompatible activities and Section 1099's prohibition against holding incompatible offices. You will need to consult your agencies' counsel regarding these and any other restrictions that may apply.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-258
Page No. 2

FACTS

You are the Division Two Director for the Yuima Municipal Water District ("YMWD") in
Pauma Valley, which is a rural agricultural area in north San Diego County. You also serve as a
Director on the Board of Rincon Ranch Community Services District ("RRCSD"). The sole
responsibility of the RRCSD is maintenance of Rincon Ranch Road in Pauma Valley.

The YMWD is seeking reimbursement from the RRCSD for the cost of road repair on
Rincon Ranch Road pursuant to installation of the water district's waterline replacement. There is
no contract between the parties obligating the RRCSD to reimburse the YMWD. Additionally, you
have an interest in a 38-acre avocado grove ("H & H Avocados") in the RRCSD and actively farm
that area. H & H Avocados is a ratepayer of the YMWD (at the standard rates applicable to all
ratepayers) and has its property assessed for road maintenance by the RRCSD (at the same ratio as
all other properties in the RRCSD).

ANALYSIS

The Acts conflict of interest provisions prohibit you from taking part in governmental
decisions in which you have a financial interest. (Section 87100.) An official has a financial interest
in a decision only if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of the
official's immediate family, or on any of the official's interests.

Interests from which a conflict of interest may arise are defined in Section 87103. Those
interests implicated by your account of the facts are the following:

• An interest in a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment of
$2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)); or in which the official is a director, officer, partner,
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (Section 87103(d)).

• An interest in real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or
more (Section 87103(b)) including a pro rata share of interests in real property of any
business entity or trust in which the individual or immediate family owns, directly,
indirectly, or beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater (Section 82033).

• An interest in a source of income to the official, or promised income, which aggregates to
$500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section 87103(c)) including a pro rata
share of the income of any business entity or trust in which the official (or his or her spouse)
owns directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a 10-percent or greater interest (Section 82030(a)).

However, as defined by the Act, income does not include "salary and reimbursement for
expenses and per diem received from state, local or federal government agency . . ." (Section
82030(b)(2).) Accordingly, the only potential interests you have identified from which a
disqualifying conflict of interest may arise are you interests in H & H Avocados as a business entity
and source income and any interest in property owned by you or H & H Avocados. Thus, we must
determine if a decision to reimburse the RRCSD may have a foreseeable and material financial
effect on these interests.

File No. A-17-258
Page No. 3

Generally, a financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or the official's agency. (Regulation 18701(a).) A financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest, and includes any governmental decision affecting a real property financial interest as described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)-(6).

Based upon the facts provided, none of your interests will be explicitly involved in decisions regarding the reimbursement for the waterline replacement. Because your interests are "not explicitly involved" in the decisions, a financial effect is reasonably foreseeable if the effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. A financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable. (Regulation 18701(b).)

A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable impact of a governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. (Regulation 18700(a).) Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an interest will be material depending on the nature of the interest.

A reasonably foreseeable effect on an interest in a business entity, including any business entity that is a source income, not explicitly involved in the decision is material "if a prudent person with sufficient information would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would contribute to a change in the price of the business entity's publicly traded stock, or the value of a privately-held business entity." (Regulations 18702.1(b) and 18703.3(a)(4).) A reasonably foreseeable effect on an interest in real property not explicitly involved in the decision is material if it meets the standard provided in Regulation 18702.2(a)(7) through (12), which include a change in the property's development potential, highest or best use, or character, as well as an influence on the market value of the property. In some circumstances, a property containing a business entity will be analyzed under the materiality standard for a business entity in 18702.1. (See Regulation 18702.2(a)(8) and (11).)

In this case, the only issue between the two agencies is whether or not the RRCSD should reimburse the YMWD for the cost of the repairs. However, determining which agency is responsible for the repairs does not have a foreseeable and material effect on wither your business or property interests.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Brian G. Lau
Assistant General Counsel

BGL:jgl

interests in an avocado farm and property within the two districts?
